div.div: Plan of Salvation Before the World Was
div.p: The great Jehovah contemplated the whole of the events connected with the earth,1 pertaining to the plan of salvation, before it rolled into existence,2 or ever “the morning stars sang together” for joy;3 the past, the present, and the future were and are, with Him, one eternal “now;”4 He knew of the fall of Adam,5 the iniquities of the antediluvians,6 of the depth of iniquity that would be connected with the human family, their weakness and strength, their power and glory, apostasies, their crimes, their righteousness and iniquity; He comprehended the fall of man,7 and his redemption; He knew the plan of salvation and pointed it out; He was acquainted with the situation of all nations and with their destiny; He ordered all things according to the council of His own will; He knows the situation of both the living and the dead,8 and has made ample provision for their redemption, according to their several circumstances, and the laws of the kingdom of God,9 whether in this world, or in the world to come.10

The idea that some men form of the justice, judgment, and mercy of God, is too foolish for an intelligent man to think of: for instance, it is common for many of our orthodox preachers to suppose that if a man is not what they call converted, if he dies in that state he must remain eternally in hell without any hope. Infinite years in torment must he spend,11 and never, never, never have an end; and yet this eternal misery12 is made frequently to rest upon the merest casualty. The breaking of a shoe-string, the tearing of a coat of those officiating, or the peculiar location in which a person lives, may be the means, indirectly of his damnation, or the cause of his not being saved. I will suppose a case which is not extraordinary: Two men, who have been equally wicked, who have neglected religion, are both of them taken sick at the same time; one of them has the good fortune to be visited by a praying man, and he gets converted a few minutes before he dies; the other sends for three different praying men, a tailor, a shoemaker, and a tinman; the tinman has a handle to solder to a can, the tailor has a buttonhole to work on some coat that he needed in a hurry, and the shoemaker has a patch to put on somebody’s boot;
